title: Coverage of essential health services
subtitle: Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.
caption:
Coverage of essential health services

Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.

The 2023 world map displays the coverage of essential health services using a color scale ranging from 0 to 100, where darker shades of blue represent higher index scores and lighter shades of green indicate lower scores. Countries in North America, Western Europe, Australia, and parts of South America, such as Brazil and Argentina, are shaded in the darkest blue, corresponding to index values between 80 and 100. In contrast, a large portion of the African continent, including nations in the central and western regions, appears in light green and pale blue tones, indicating scores generally falling between 20 and 60. Parts of Asia, including India and Indonesia, show mid-range values represented by medium blue shades, typically situated between 40 and 60. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching to signify that no data is available for that region. The index assesses coverage based on risk-standardized death rates and the implementation of common interventions across a spectrum of services including health promotion, prevention, treatment, rehabilitation, and palliation. Tracer indicators are utilized to represent the overall coverage of these essential services, resulting in a global distribution where high-income regions consistently occupy the upper end of the 0 to 100 scale while many low-income regions cluster in the lower to middle bands.
